elements.sent-2411-001: Advancements in large language models (LLMs) have renewed concerns about AI alignment—the consistency between human and AI goals and values.
elements.sent-2411-002: As various jurisdictions enact legislation on AI safety, the concept of alignment must be defined and measured across different domains.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper proposes an experimental framework to assess whether LLMs adhere to ethical and legal standards in the relatively unexplored context of finance.
elements.sent-2411-004: We prompt nine LLMs to impersonate the CEO of a financial institution and test their willingness to misuse customer assets to repay outstanding corporate debt.
elements.sent-2411-005: Beginning with a baseline configuration, we adjust preferences, incentives and constraints, analyzing the impact of each adjustment with logistic regression.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our findings reveal significant heterogeneity in the baseline propensity for unethical behavior of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-007: Factors such as risk aversion, profit expectations, and regulatory environment consistently influence misalignment in ways predicted by economic theory, although the magnitude of these effects varies across LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-008: This paper highlights both the benefits and limitations of simulation-based, ex post safety testing.
elements.sent-2411-009: While it can inform financial authorities and institutions aiming to ensure LLM safety, there is a clear trade-off between generality and cost.
elements.sent-2411-010: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly transforming how we approach problems across various domains, thanks to their improved natural language understanding~\citep{min2023recent and their advanced reasoning capabilities~\citep{wei2022chain,huang2023towards .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Financial firms, known for being early adopters of new technologies, have already integrated LLMs into their operations to varying extents~\citep{ati2024impact,msv2024ai,davenport2023gpt .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The same flexibility and autonomy that make these models so powerful also introduce significant challenges to their practical applicability.
elements.sent-2411-013: Due to their complex architectures, LLMs are prone to issues like hallucinations~\citep{ji2023survey and biases~\citep{gallegos2024bias , which can result in unintended consequences when deployed in real-world applications.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Insecure, malfunctioning, or misguided AI can impact financial stability and market fairness and transparency, while also facilitating criminal abuse of the financial system~\citep{danielsson2023use .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Understanding how undesirable AI behavior may arise, and how to prevent it, is of paramount importance.

Our goal is to measure the likelihood of the agent choosing to misuse customer funds in violation of existing regulations and ethical standards. } \label{fig:experiment-diagram}
elements.sent-2411-016: Existing work primarily addresses these challenges by developing models that prioritize safety \citep{bai2022training , and introducing guardrails to prevent the generation of harmful content \citep{zeng2024shieldgemma, inan2023llama .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Several studies have established benchmarks to evaluate the safety of LLMs in generating illegal or violent content~\citep{tedeschi2024alert , as well as their robustness against ``jailbreak'' attacks, which can cause models to still produce unwanted content despite the presence of guardrails or safety features~\citep{chao2024jailbreakbench .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recently, more attention has been devoted to the tension between maximizing rewards and behaving ethically that may affect LLMs in some situations~\citep{pan2023rewards .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Nevertheless, most benchmarks and experiments focus on broad, general ethical concepts, with a lack of domain-specific evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-020: With the introduction of novel laws and frameworks on AI~\cite{whitehouse2023,euai2024 , it has become increasingly necessary to study and operationalize these standards within specialized domains.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Our paper presents a thorough exploration and study of the LLM alignment problem in the financial sector, which has received only limited attention despite its critical implications.
elements.sent-2411-022: In detail, we propose a comprehensive simulation study to assess the likelihood that several recent LLMs may deviate from ethical and lawful financial behavior.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our simulated environment, shown in Figure~\ref{fig:experiment-diagram , is based on the collapse of the cryptoasset exchange FTX, described as ``one of the largest financial frauds in history''~\citep{ftx-fraud-article .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, we prompt the models to impersonate the CEO of a financial institution and test whether they would misappropriate customer assets to cover internal losses, given various internal and external factors.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-026: We develop a novel simulation environment to assess the alignment of LLMs in the financial sector, which can be easily adapted to address different concerns.
elements.sent-2411-027: We evaluate our framework using nine LLMs, varying in size and capabilities, and conducting approximately 54,000 simulations per model.
elements.sent-2411-028: We establish a robust statistical framework to assess the propensity of the models to engage in fraudulent behavior in relation to different incentives and constraints.
elements.sent-2411-029: We release the code and benchmark data, which will be publicly available on GitHub~\footnote{Link released upon acceptance.. .}
elements.sent-2411-030: We believe our work provides a solid foundation for future research on the alignment of LLMs in the financial sector.
elements.sent-2411-031: Additionally, it can assist financial authorities and institutions in better understanding and measuring the risks associated with the adoption of these models.
elements.sent-2411-032: Alignment, as defined by \cite{wang2018glue , refers to ensuring that an AI system’s actions remain consistent with the intended goals set by human operators.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In a recent comprehensive survey, \cite{ji2023survey partition alignment research into two sub-fields: forward alignment which focuses on how to train AI systems to maximize alignment with a given set of values, and backward alignment aiming at gathering evidence on the alignment of existing AIs (evaluation), and governing any emerging misalignment.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The method and experiments proposed in this paper fall into the second sub-field.
elements.sent-2411-035: Several studies have already highlighted the gap between a model’s performance on benchmark tasks and its ability to adhere to desirable behaviors in uncontrolled environments~\citep{bisk2020experience .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Thus, recent research has shifted towards incorporating safety, ethics, and value alignment as core evaluation dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-037: From an economic or financial perspective, nascent literature is exploring to which extent LLMs' behavior replicates homo economicus\footnote{A rational agent who optimizes their choices based on personal preferences and external constraints}\citep{ross2024llm , whether LLMs can emulate non-rational choices~\citep{coletta2024llm , and whether insights from economics can help in modeling interactions between humans and LLMs~\citep{immorlica2024generative .}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: This body of literature suggests that we may not be far from leveraging LLM models within companies to support and help make informed decisions.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our paper draws significantly on the ideas and experimental framework presented in \cite{scheurer2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-040: The authors assess whether an LLM impersonating a stock trader is willing to act on insider information, despite being told that such behavior should be avoided.
elements.sent-2411-041: They find that the LLM indeed engages in insider trading if given the right incentives.
elements.sent-2411-042: To the best of our knowledge, \cite{scheurer2024large is the only existing systematic evaluation of LLM alignment in a financial setting.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Our work builds on this approach by examining LLM behavior in a different financial scenario inspired by real-world events.
elements.sent-2411-044: While the authors focus primarily on GPT-4 variants, we evaluate a diverse set of both closed and open-source LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-045: Furthermore, we extend their methodology by introducing a multidimensional ``pressure vector'' that systematically varies prompt components, allowing for a more granular analysis of factors influencing model decisions.
elements.sent-2411-046: In designing our experiment, we also draw on \cite{shanahan2023role , who introduce the concept of “role play” to characterize human-like behavior of LLM-based dialogue agents ''without falling into the trap of anthropomorphism''.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The scenario underlying our simulation is inspired by the 2022 collapse of the cryptoasset exchange FTX~\citep{ftx-fraud-article .}
elements.sent-2411-048: We model an imaginary financial firm where an LLM-powered agent acts as the CEO.
elements.sent-2411-049: This agent is faced with the dilemma of either risking the firm's financial failure or misusing customer funds to ensure the exchange's continued operations.
elements.sent-2411-050: We employ a fictional, but plausible, setting to avoid data contamination, given that some of the examined LLMs have knowledge cutoffs after November 2022, when FTX ceased operations.
elements.sent-2411-051: A schematic view of our experimental framework is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:experiment-diagram .}
elements.sent-2411-052: We first design a system prompt to provide the LLM agent with basic information about its persona and the company: he's Naga, the CEO of the seashell trading firm.
elements.sent-2411-053: Then we execute a second prompt to inform the LLM agent that the company's financial situation has taken a turn for the worst, and the head of the trading division (Ursus) requests to use customer money to cover the loan.
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally, we ask the LLM agent to make a decision on how to answer Ursus' request: deny, partially approve or fully approve the loan.
elements.sent-2411-055: Given the system prompt setup where the role of the LLM agent is of a custodian, being set only to use funds to fulfill customer orders, we consider full or partial approval as misconduct (misaligned) and the denial of the request as the only legal scenario (aligned).
elements.sent-2411-056: In this framework, the CEO is modeled as a fully rational agent maximizing personal satisfaction based on (i) individual preferences, (ii) stochastic external events, and (iii) external constraints and incentive schemes.
elements.sent-2411-057: Building on the concept of exerting "pressure" as outlined in \citep{scheurer2024large , we parameterize the simulation to assess how the agent responds to various incentives and constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-058: For simplicity, we refer to these parameters collectively as pressure variables throughout the remainder of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-059: We test each LLM model against several variations of the simulation by systematically altering the prompts using placeholders that adjust the pressure settings.
elements.sent-2411-060: These settings represent different environmental and agent characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-061: Figure~\ref{fig:experiment-diagram shows the seven variables we modify.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Appendix \ref{app:prompts provides a full description of the prompts, and Appendix \ref{app:factors lists the corresponding pressure variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Our experimental setup is inspired by a standard framework in economic theory: constrained optimization under uncertainty.

elements.sent-2411-064: We introduce seven variables to define the LLM agent and the environment, with two variations for each around a baseline.

elements.sent-2411-066: We consider the following domains: for the LLM agent, risk aversion, trust in trading branch capabilities, and personal outlook on the future; for the environment, market conditions, regulation, corporate governance, and the value of loans owed to external lenders.

elements.sent-2411-069: We generate a total of 2,187 possible simulation configurations, accounting for every combination of the three values (positive pressure, negative pressure, and the baseline) across the seven pressure variables.
elements.sent-2411-070: To interpret the LLM responses under different pressure conditions, we fit the data using a logistic regression model.
elements.sent-2411-071: Specifically, for each LLM $n$, we represent the probability of misalignment $p_n$ as a function of the two modalities $x_{i+}$ and $x_{i-}$ (either zero or one) of the seven pressure variables $i\in{1, \dots, 7}$, yielding models of the form: \begin{equation}\ln \left(\frac{p^{n}}{1 - p^{n}}\right) = \beta_0^n + \sum_{i=1}^7 \beta_{i+}^n x_{i+}^n + \sum_{i=1}^7 \beta_{i-}^n x_{i-}^n. \label{eq:logistic-regression}\end{equation} Importantly, the intercepts $\beta_0^n$ are necessary to correctly interpolate the different baseline probabilities observed across models, while the independent treatment of the ``positive'' ($x_{i+}$) and ``negative'' ($x_{i-}$) pressure variables is necessary in order to correctly measure the potentially asymmetric effect that the two modalities can have on the LLM propensity to misalign.
elements.sent-2411-072: The models are fitted by maximum likelihood, which allows for the estimation of asymptotic values of errors and p-values for the parameters $\beta^n_{i}$.
elements.sent-2411-073: In turn, these parameters are used to quantify and compare the pressure exerted by a specific variable on the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-074: In Appendix \ref{app:additional-results , we check the robustness of the logistic regression results by showing that an ordinal logistic model and an RNN model yield qualitatively equivalent outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-075: For the sake of generalization of the results and of the subsequent discussion, we evaluated different LLMs both open and closed source.

elements.section-2411-011: Robustness checks on the logistic regression results
elements.sent-2411-228: In this work, we have interpolated the decision-making of LLMs using logistic regression models.
elements.sent-2411-229: In this Appendix we show that interpolating the same data using other models of increased complexity leads to equivalent results, thus supporting the simple model choice presented in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-230: Specifically, we here confront the results shown in the main text with those obtained via an ordinal logistic regression and via an autoregressive logistic regression implemented via a recurrent neural network (RNN).
elements.sent-2411-231: In the main text, we have presented results obtained using a logistic regression fit on data with the two misalignment choices of a partial approval and a full approval of the loan were aggregated into a single variable tracking the occurrence of a misaligned decision.
elements.sent-2411-232: We repeated the regression on a dataset with both choices using an ordinal logistic regression model, where the partial approval is considered to be a misalignment of lower entity.
elements.sent-2411-233: The regression yields results that are qualitatively equivalent to those presented in the main text, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:parameters-lr-olr-rnn and in Table~\ref{tab:ordinal-lr-params .}}
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.32\linewidth]{figures/appendix/lr_params.png} \includegraphics[width=0.32\linewidth]{figures/appendix/olr_params.png} \includegraphics[width=0.32\linewidth]{figures/appendix/rnn_params.png} \caption{ Parameters compared across regression models . A comparison of the parameters obtained for the different variables when fitting the data using three distinct models: the plain logistic regression model discussed in the main text (left), an ordinal logistic regression model fitted with partial and full misalignment data (centre), and an `autoregressive' logistic regression model built using an RNN approach. Top and bottom rows present the parameters expected to have a positive and negative sign respectively. } \label{fig:parameters-lr-olr-rnn}
elements.sent-2411-234: We hypothesize that the autoregressive nature of LLMs implies that, generally speaking, dependencies may exist among the variables, even with respect to the order in which they are presented in the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-235: To strengthen our results, we repeated the regression exercise using an autoregressive extension of logistic regression and confirmed that the qualitative outcomes were equivalent to the original results.
elements.sent-2411-236: Specifically, we used a recurrent neural network (RNN) implementing the following operations.
elements.sent-2411-237: First, the input variables are passed through passed through a fully connected layer with a one-dimensional output.
elements.sent-2411-238: Then, this one-dimensional output is summed to the one-dimensional hidden space (a kind of ``misalignment state'') and passed to a tanh activation function to generate a new hidden space.
elements.sent-2411-239: Finally, the misalignment state is multiplied by a parameter and passed through a sigmoid function to predict the misalignment probability.
elements.sent-2411-240: An illustration of this architecture is provided in Figure \ref{fig:rnn-illustration .}
elements.sent-2411-241: We train the network's parameters using a cross-entropy loss between the misalignment decision made by the LLM and the final predicted misalignment probability $p_7$.
elements.sent-2411-242: We train for each model for 20 epochs using a batch size of 32, an Adam optimizer and a weight decay of $10^{-4}$.
elements.sent-2411-243: This model, which we can consider a kind of ``autoregressive logistic regression'', yields results that are qualitatively equivalent to those presented in the main text, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:parameters-lr-olr-rnn and in Table~\ref{tab:rnn-params .}}
elements.sent-2411-244: The RNNs model the probability of misalignment as a function of the prompt variable and the previously computed hidden misalignment state.
elements.sent-2411-245: The marginal effect that each prompt variable has on the probability of misalignment is depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:rnn-responses for a subset of models.}
elements.sent-2411-246: The figure illustrates the different baseline propensities to misalign across models, as well as the asymmetric effect that each prompt variable can have on $p$.
